growth_form: Erect thorny shrub 30–100 cm. Leaves pinnately compound, 5–7 ovate toothed leaflets. Flowers solitary or few, 5-petaled, deep pink, 3–5 cm. Hip 1–2 cm, red, fleshy, persistent into winter.
habitat: Margins of shrub tundra, riverbanks, and disturbed ground. Scattered along drainage channels and sheltered slopes near Tuktoyaktuk; more common south toward treeline.
distinguishing_features: The only wild rose in the region. Dense fine prickles covering stems, large pink flowers, and bright red hips are collectively unmistakable.
cultural_notes: Hips are among the richest plant sources of vitamin C; eaten fresh, dried, or brewed as tea. Fibrous seeds and inner hairs should be removed before eating.

photos.attribution: (c) keairareischauer, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Low rhizomatous herb 5–20 cm. Leaves 4–6, whorled near stem apex, ovate with prominent parallel veins, 2–4 cm. Inflorescence a compact cluster of tiny purplish flowers subtended by 4 showy white bracts. Fruit a tight cluster of bright red drupes.
habitat: Moist to mesic tundra, heath, and shrubby habitats. Occurs in moss-rich tundra and open woodland edges near the Mackenzie Delta; Low Arctic and subarctic.
distinguishing_features: The 4 whorled leaves with parallel venation and 4-white-bracted flower head are unmistakable. Bright red berry clusters ripen late summer.
cultural_notes: Berries edible though bland and mealy; eaten fresh or mixed with other berries. Consumed across the circumpolar region.

photos.attribution: (c) Alexander Yakovlev, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Alexander Yakovlev

growth_form: Upright shrub 1–4 m. Leaves alternate, oval to lanceolate, 3–7 cm, densely silvery-scaly on both surfaces. Flowers small, tubular, 4-lobed, pale yellow, intensely fragrant. Fruit a silvery mealy drupe ~1 cm with a large stone.
habitat: Open shrub tundra, riverbanks, and gravelly disturbed sites. Reaches the southern fringe of the ISR along river corridors; more common near and south of treeline along the Mackenzie.
distinguishing_features: Entire plant appears metallic-silver from dense stellate scales. Intensely sweet floral fragrance detectable at a distance. No spines; leaves superficially resemble willows but the silvery sheen is unique.
cultural_notes: Berries edible but dry and mealy with a large stone limiting palatability. Seeds used as beads in some traditions.

photos.attribution: (c) Eric Lamb, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Eric Lamb

growth_form: Small perennial herb 5–30 cm. Basal leaves kidney- to heart-shaped, 1–4 cm wide, long-petioled. Flowers tiny, reddish-green, in a branched raceme. Fruit a flat winged achene, turning deep red at maturity.
habitat: Snowbeds, moist rocky slopes, stream margins, and disturbed tundra. One of the most common and widespread Arctic herbs; found throughout the ISR on any moist sheltered site.
distinguishing_features: Kidney-shaped basal leaves with a pronounced sour taste are diagnostic. Reddish winged achenes turn the entire inflorescence wine-red in late summer.
cultural_notes: Leaves eaten raw or cooked; one of the most important Arctic edible plants due to high vitamin C content. Sour taste from oxalic acid—consumed in moderation.

photos.attribution: (c) ahmedm, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by ahmedm

growth_form: Erect perennial herb 20–60 cm. Leaves pinnately compound, 9–17 oval leaflets. Flowers pea-like, pink-purple, raceme of 10–30 blossoms. Pods segmented (loments), 2–5 segments, slightly winged. Roots thick, fleshy, sweet-tasting.
habitat: Gravel bars, riverbanks, sandy tundra, and open shrubby slopes. Common throughout the ISR on well-drained substrates; characteristic of Mackenzie Delta gravel bars.
distinguishing_features: Pea-family flower structure with a pink-purple raceme. Distinguish from toxic Astragalus spp. by segmented loment pods rather than inflated pods. Roots sweet-smelling.
cultural_notes: Roots a major traditional food across the western Arctic; eaten raw, boiled, or dried. Caution: do not confuse with Hedysarum mackenzii (white to pale pink flowers), which is poisonous.

photos.attribution: (c) Donna Pomeroy, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Donna Pomeroy

growth_form: Low stoloniferous perennial forming dense mats. Leaves pinnately compound, 5–25 cm, 7–25 toothed leaflets alternating large and small, silvery-silky beneath. Flowers solitary on long stalks, bright yellow, 5-petaled, 1.5–2.5 cm.
habitat: Lakeshores, river beaches, moist disturbed ground, and coastal flats. Abundant along the coast near Tuktoyaktuk and throughout the Mackenzie Delta on moist, often slightly saline substrates.
distinguishing_features: Silvery silky-hairy leaf undersides are diagnostic. Spreads by long red runners like a strawberry. Large yellow flowers on leafless stalks among the pinnate leaves.
cultural_notes: Thick starchy roots eaten raw or cooked; one of the most nutritious Arctic root vegetables. Young leaves and runners also edible.

photos.attribution: (c) Zdeňka Nováková, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Zdeňka Nováková

growth_form: Slender perennial herb 10–30 cm. Basal leaves lanceolate to oblong, 3–10 cm, margins slightly recurved. Inflorescence a dense spike bearing tiny white to pale pink flowers above and reddish-brown bulbils below.
habitat: Mesic to moist tundra and heath throughout the Low Arctic. One of the most abundant and widespread tundra forbs in the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Reddish-brown bulbils in the lower half of the inflorescence are diagnostic and unique in the region. Narrow white flower spike above a basal rosette of lanceolate leaves.
cultural_notes: Bulbils and young leaves eaten raw or cooked. Bulbils have a mildly nutty flavour.

photos.attribution: (c) trowbric, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by trowbric

growth_form: Tall perennial herb 40–150 cm. Leaves alternate, lanceolate, 5–15 cm, with a pale midrib. Flowers in a tall raceme, 4-petaled, bright magenta-pink, 2–3 cm. Narrow pods split to release seeds on silky white plumes.
habitat: Disturbed ground, burned areas, gravel bars, and roadsides. Among the first colonizers after disturbance; widespread throughout the ISR wherever soil is exposed.
distinguishing_features: Tall, showy magenta flower spikes unmistakable in late summer. Pale midrib and alternate leaves diagnostic. Drifting white seed plumes conspicuous from late August onward.
cultural_notes: Young spring shoots eaten like asparagus; inner pith of older stems eaten raw. Young leaves and flowers used for tea. One of the most widely used edible plants across the North.

photos.attribution: (c) Anton Dangl, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Anton Dangl

growth_form: Perennial herb; basal leaves developing after flowering, palmately lobed, 10–40 cm wide, white-woolly beneath. Flowering stems 10–30 cm, appearing before leaves in early spring, bearing heads of small white to pinkish flowers. Dioecious.
habitat: Wet sedge meadows, snowbed margins, streambanks, and disturbed tundra. Very common throughout the ISR, especially in low moist areas.
distinguishing_features: Flowering stalks emerge before leaves in spring—one of the earliest blooms in the Arctic. Large lobed leaves with white-woolly undersides distinctive later in season.
cultural_notes: Young leaves and flowering stalks eaten as a cooked green. Leaves traditionally dried and burned as a salt substitute.

photos.attribution: (c) christinelind, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Low to medium shrub 20–100 cm. Twigs with small resinous glands. Leaves small, 1–3 cm, round to broadly obovate, crenate-serrate, turning brilliant orange-red in autumn. Erect catkins 1–2 cm. Bark reddish-brown.
habitat: Wet to mesic tundra, polygon bogs, and heath throughout the Low Arctic. One of the most abundant and characteristic shrubs of the ISR; dominant in many shrub tundra communities.
distinguishing_features: Small, round, gland-dotted leaves with blunt teeth on reddish-brown twigs. Brilliant scarlet-orange autumn colour makes this the most conspicuous shrub in fall across the tundra.

photos.attribution: (c) Dustin Snider, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Dustin Snider

growth_form: Prostrate to low mat-forming shrub, stems rarely exceeding 10–15 cm in height, spreading widely. Leaves elliptic to obovate, 1–4 cm, glossy above, silvery below, margins entire or obscurely toothed. Catkins erect, 1–4 cm, silky.
habitat: Mesic to dry tundra, ridge tops, and exposed slopes throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common on well-drained mineral soils throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Prostrate habit with glossy green upper and silvery lower leaf surfaces. Upright fuzzy catkins on a ground-hugging plant are characteristic. Smaller and more prostrate than most other willows.
cultural_notes: Young catkins eaten raw in spring as a traditional snack; mildly sweet.

photos.attribution: (c) Mashuri Waite, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Mashuri Waite

growth_form: Low to medium erect or ascending shrub 30–100 cm. Leaves narrowly elliptic to lanceolate, 2–6 cm, dark green and slightly glossy above, paler below, margins entire or obscurely toothed. Catkins 2–4 cm, appearing with leaves.
habitat: Moist shrub tundra, polygon margins, and riverbanks throughout the Low Arctic coastal plain. One of the most common and often dominant willows near Tuktoyaktuk.
distinguishing_features: Narrow, entire, dark green leaves with pale undersides. Medium height among local willows. Distinguished from S. richardsonii by narrower leaves and less woolly catkins.

photos.attribution: (c) David McCorquodale, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by David McCorquodale

growth_form: Creeping dwarf shrub forming mats close to the ground, stems rarely over 5 cm tall. Leaves round to broadly elliptic, 1–3 cm, with conspicuously impressed net venation above, whitish or bluish-green below, margins entire. Catkins erect, 1–3 cm.
habitat: Rocky tundra, dry slopes, and ridge tops throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common on well-drained gravelly sites throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Conspicuously net-veined, round leaves with deeply impressed veins and whitish surface below are unmistakable. Creeping mat habit. No other willow in the region has such strongly reticulate leaves.

photos.attribution: (c) Andy Fyon, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Andy Fyon

growth_form: Low to medium spreading shrub 30–80 cm. Leaves broadly elliptic to obovate, 2–5 cm, surface wrinkled, dark green above, glaucous and densely white-hairy below. Catkins large, 3–6 cm, densely silky-hairy, appearing before or with leaves.
habitat: Wet to moist tundra, polygon margins, and lake shores throughout the Low Arctic coastal plain. Characteristic of the Mackenzie Delta and coastal areas near Tuktoyaktuk.
distinguishing_features: Large woolly catkins appearing early in spring are conspicuous. Broad, wrinkled leaves with densely white-hairy undersides distinguish it from S. pulchra. One of the broader-leaved willows of the Low Arctic.

photos.attribution: (c) M. Torre Jorgenson, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by M. Torre Jorgenson

growth_form: Multi-stemmed shrub 20–80 cm with shreddy brown bark. Leaves pinnately compound, 3–7 small, silky-hairy leaflets. Flowers bright yellow, 5-petaled, 1.5–2.5 cm, resembling a small wild rose. Long-flowering through summer.
habitat: Moist to mesic tundra, fens, and rocky slopes throughout the Low Arctic. Common throughout the ISR on a wide range of substrates.
distinguishing_features: The only native shrub with bright yellow rose-like flowers in the region. Shreddy brown bark on older stems combined with the long flowering season is diagnostic.

photos.attribution: (c) Олексій, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Олексій

growth_form: Low mat-forming dwarf shrub 3–15 cm. Leaves small, 1–3 cm, oblong, dark glossy green above, white-woolly below, margins entire to slightly revolute. Flowers solitary, white, 8-petaled, 2–3 cm. Achenes with long, feathery, twisted styles.
habitat: Dry to mesic tundra, rocky ridges, and beach ridges throughout the Low and High Arctic. Dominant on well-drained tundra throughout the ISR; forms extensive mats on pingos and ridge crests.
distinguishing_features: White 8-petaled flowers on bare peduncles above a mat of small dark glossy leaves with woolly white undersides. Feathery, twisted seed heads conspicuous in late summer. Entire leaf margins distinguish it from D. octopetala.

photos.attribution: (c) Shawn Billerman, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Shawn Billerman

growth_form: Low mat-forming evergreen dwarf shrub 10–30 cm. Stems densely covered in 4 rows of tiny scale-like leaves pressed tightly against the stem, creating a 4-angled rope-like appearance. Flowers bell-shaped, white, nodding, 5–8 mm, solitary on slender red peduncles.
habitat: Mesic to dry heath tundra throughout the Low and High Arctic. Among the most abundant shrubs of the ISR; dominant in heath communities on well-drained slopes.
distinguishing_features: Rope-like stems with four rows of overlapping scale leaves giving a distinctly 4-angled profile. No other tundra plant resembles it. Nodding white bell flowers on red stalks.

photos.attribution: (c) Paul Sokoloff, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA), uploaded by Paul Sokoloff

growth_form: Low wiry evergreen shrub 10–40 cm. Leaves alternate, linear-lanceolate, 1–3 cm, dark green and leathery above, white-glaucous below, margins strongly revolute. Flowers urn-shaped, pink, 5–8 mm, in small terminal clusters.
habitat: Sphagnum bogs, wet heaths, and polygon bogs throughout the Low Arctic. Common in wet, acidic tundra throughout the ISR, especially in Sphagnum-dominated habitats.
distinguishing_features: Narrow strap-like leaves with strongly rolled-under margins and a brilliant white underside are diagnostic. Pink urn-shaped flowers resemble a small blueberry. All parts toxic.
cultural_notes: Toxic—do not ingest. Contains grayanotoxins. Not to be confused with edible ericaceous shrubs.

photos.attribution: (c) Jari Kontiokorpi, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Jari Kontiokorpi

growth_form: Low sprawling evergreen shrub 15–50 cm. Leaves linear to narrowly elliptic, 1–3 cm, leathery, dark green above, densely rust-woolly below, strongly aromatic when crushed, margins revolute. Flowers white, 5-petaled, in terminal clusters.
habitat: Sphagnum bogs, wet heath, and moist polygon tundra throughout the Low Arctic. Common in acidic poorly drained substrates throughout the ISR; often associated with Sphagnum and Eriophorum.
distinguishing_features: Narrow leathery leaves with rolled-under margins and thick rust-brown woolly underside coating. Strong resinous aromatic odour when leaves are crushed. Distinguished from R. groenlandicum by narrower leaves.
cultural_notes: Leaves traditionally brewed as tea. Consumed in moderation; contains compounds potentially toxic in excess.

photos.attribution: (c) nkoroleva, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Erect to spreading shrub 1–3 m. Leaves broadly ovate, 3–8 cm, sharply doubly-toothed, sticky when young, bright green both surfaces. Male catkins elongate, pendulous; female catkins small, becoming persistent woody cone-like strobiles 1–2 cm.
habitat: Riverbanks, shrub tundra, and south-facing slopes at the tundra–boreal interface. Found along river drainages flowing toward the Mackenzie Delta; reaches the southern fringe of the ISR.
distinguishing_features: The largest-statured native shrub in the region. Woody persistent cone-like strobiles on branches year-round are unmistakable. Sticky, bright-green doubly-serrate leaves unlike all local willows and birches.

photos.attribution: (c) Christopher J. Earle, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Christopher J. Earle

growth_form: Dense cushion-forming perennial 2–8 cm tall, spreading to 30+ cm. Leaves tiny, linear, 5–12 mm, crowded, bright green. Flowers pink (rarely white), 5-petaled, 8–12 mm, solitary on very short stalks, often covering the entire cushion in full bloom.
habitat: Exposed rocky ridges, gravelly slopes, and dry tundra throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common on well-drained exposed mineral soils throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Tight bright-green moss-like cushion smothered in pink flowers is unmistakable in bloom. Long-lived; large cushions may be centuries old.

photos.attribution: (c) Katrin Simon, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Katrin Simon

growth_form: Mat-forming perennial with trailing stems, 2–10 cm tall. Leaves opposite, tiny, ovate, 2–5 mm, arranged in 4 ranks along stems, with a lime-secreting pit at the tip. Flowers deep purple-pink, 5-petaled, 1–1.5 cm, solitary.
habitat: Rocky slopes, scree, gravelly tundra, and exposed ridges throughout the Low and High Arctic. One of the earliest and most common flowering plants in the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Earliest-flowering plant in the Arctic, often blooming at snowmelt. Deep purple flowers on a low mat with tiny opposite 4-ranked leaves are unmistakable.

photos.attribution: (c) Anthony B. Zerafa, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Anthony B. Zerafa

growth_form: Slender erect herb 5–20 cm. Basal leaves kidney-shaped, palmately 3–7 lobed, long-petioled. Stem leaves smaller, bearing reddish-brown bulbils in their axils. Typically one terminal white flower, 5-petaled, 1–1.5 cm; often absent, reproduction mainly by bulbils.
habitat: Moist rocky slopes, cliffs, and snowbed margins throughout the Low and High Arctic. Scattered in sheltered moist microsites throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Reddish-brown bulbils in leaf axils along the stem are diagnostic and unique among local saxifrages. The single nodding terminal white flower (when present) confirms ID.

photos.attribution: (c) Piotr Lukasik, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Piotr Lukasik

growth_form: Low perennial herb 5–20 cm from a dense caudex covered in old leaf bases. Leaves basal, pinnately lobed, 2–8 cm, densely hairy. Flowering stems leafless, hairy. Flowers solitary, yellow (rarely white), 4-petaled, 2–4 cm. Capsule oval, bristly.
habitat: Dry to mesic gravelly tundra, ridge tops, and beach ridges. Widespread on well-drained substrates throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Solitary yellow flower on a hairy leafless stem above a basal rosette of pinnately lobed leaves. Flower bud nods, then straightens as it opens. The only native poppy in the region.

photos.attribution: (c) brianbuma, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Low succulent perennial herb 5–30 cm. Leaves basal and on stem, kidney- to heart-shaped, 3–10 cm wide, glossy, margins crenate. Flowers bright yellow, 5–8 petal-like sepals, 2–4 cm; no true petals.
habitat: Streambanks, lake margins, wet sedge meadows, and flooded tundra throughout the Low Arctic. Common in wet habitats throughout the ISR, especially near water.
distinguishing_features: Bright yellow flowers and glossy kidney-shaped leaves in wet habitats are unmistakable. Among the showiest of the early-spring tundra flowers.
cultural_notes: Raw plant toxic due to protoanemonin. Young leaves consumed only after thorough boiling with multiple water changes in some Indigenous traditions.

photos.attribution: (c) eeyipes, some rights reserved (CC BY)

growth_form: Slender perennial herb 5–20 cm. Basal leaves long-petioled, 3-parted, leaflets wedge-shaped and deeply lobed. A whorl of 3 similar sessile involucral leaves mid-stem. Flower solitary, white above, often bluish-tinged below, 1–2 cm. Achene head spherical, woolly.
habitat: Moist to mesic tundra, shrubby slopes, and streambanks throughout the Low Arctic. Common throughout the ISR in moderately moist to well-drained habitats.
distinguishing_features: White flower with a bluish tinge below, solitary on a slender stem with a characteristic whorl of 3 divided leaves at mid-stem. Woolly spherical seed head later in season.

photos.attribution: (c) pbulman, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Perennial herb 10–35 cm. Leaves pinnately lobed with toothed segments, basal and alternate on stem. Flowers pink to purple, bilabiate, in a dense terminal spike; upper lip hooded, lower lip 3-lobed. Hemiparasitic on grass roots.
habitat: Wet sedge meadows, polygon bogs, and moist tundra throughout the Low Arctic. Common in wet coastal habitats throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Fern-like pinnately divided leaves with a terminal spike of pink hooded flowers. Distinguished from P. lanata by pink (not white-woolly) inflorescence and less conspicuously hairy bracts.

photos.attribution: (c) Mihail Knjasev, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Mihail Knjasev

growth_form: Low perennial herb 5–20 cm. Leaves pinnately lobed, basal and few on stem. Flowers pink, bilabiate, clustered in a dense spike; inflorescence bracts and calyx covered in conspicuous long white woolly hairs. Hemiparasitic.
habitat: Moist to mesic tundra and heath throughout the Low Arctic. Common throughout the ISR on a range of substrates.
distinguishing_features: Densely white-woolly inflorescence is immediately distinctive—the long hairs nearly obscure the pink flowers, giving a 'fluffy white cap' appearance unlike any other plant in the region.

photos.attribution: (c) Игорь Поспелов, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Tall annual or biennial herb 20–80 cm. Leaves alternate, lanceolate, 5–15 cm, toothed, woolly when young. Stem hollow, somewhat succulent. Flower heads numerous, yellow, with ray florets. At maturity, seeds bear fluffy white pappus producing a conspicuous cotton-ball appearance.
habitat: Disturbed wet ground, muddy lake shores, flooded polygon margins, and roadsides throughout the Low Arctic. An opportunist on seasonally flooded or disturbed substrates; common throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Tall coarse woolly herb with yellow daisy-like heads turning to globose white cotton-ball seed heads. Hollow stem and preference for disturbed wet ground are characteristic. Among the tallest forbs in Low Arctic habitats.

photos.attribution: (c) csledge, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by csledge

growth_form: Aquatic or semi-aquatic perennial. Leaves trifoliate, leaflets oval 4–8 cm, on long petioles rising from a thick creeping rhizome. Flower stalk 15–30 cm bearing a raceme of 10–20 white, heavily fringed (fimbriate) flowers with a pink tinge, 1–1.5 cm.
habitat: Shallow ponds, lakes, bogs, and Sphagnum fens throughout the Low Arctic. Common in shallow standing water throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Trifoliate leaves resembling a large clover rising from water or wet mud. Heavily fringed white flower petals are unlike any other aquatic plant in the region.
cultural_notes: Rhizomes edible but very bitter; require extensive processing. Used medicinally as a digestive bitter in some traditions.

photos.attribution: (c) Milli Vedder, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Milli Vedder

growth_form: Rhizomatous perennial sedge 20–60 cm. Stems round in cross-section. Leaves narrow, 2–6 mm wide, channelled, triangular-tipped. Inflorescence of 2–8 drooping spikelets on unequal stalks, each becoming a conspicuous white cotton-ball of silky bristles at fruiting.
habitat: Wet sedge meadows, fens, lake margins, and polygon bogs throughout the Low Arctic. Among the most abundant plants in the ISR, often forming vast stands in wet tundra.
distinguishing_features: Multiple (2–8) nodding silky-white cotton heads on a single stem distinguish this from E. vaginatum (single head). A defining visual feature of the summer tundra landscape.
cultural_notes: Cotton used as wick material for qulliq (oil lamps). Young shoots edible.

photos.attribution: (c) Bogdan V. Kryzhatyuk, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA), uploaded by Bogdan V. Kryzhatyuk

growth_form: Densely tufted perennial sedge forming large tussocks 20–50 cm tall and up to 60 cm diameter. Leaves very narrow, 1–2 mm, 3-angled. Each stem bears a single terminal white cotton head. Upper stem sheaths bladeless and inflated.
habitat: Wet to moist tundra, polygon bogs, and acidic fens throughout the Low Arctic. Dominant in many Low Arctic tundra communities and the characteristic tussock-forming plant across the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Single white cotton head per stem and the distinctive large tussock growth habit. The hummocky tussock tundra landscape is largely defined by this species. Tussocks may be several decades old.
cultural_notes: Tussock cotton used as lamp wick material similarly to E. angustifolium.

photos.attribution: (c) dhope, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Rhizomatous perennial sedge 30–80 cm, forming dense stands. Leaves flat, 3–7 mm wide, green to yellowish-green. Inflorescence of 2–4 slender erect to nodding pistillate spikes and 1–3 staminate spikes at stem tip. Scales dark brown.
habitat: Wet sedge meadows, lake margins, polygon fens, and shallow water throughout the Low Arctic. One of the most abundant and dominant sedges of ISR wetlands.
distinguishing_features: Tall, densely growing sedge with multiple cylindrical dark brown spikes on a triangular stem. Dominance in wet meadows makes it likely the sedge encountered in most wet tundra habitats; definitive ID requires hand lens.

photos.attribution: (c) Шильников Дмитрий Сергеевич, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Шильников Дмитрий Сергеевич

growth_form: Stiff rhizomatous perennial sedge 10–40 cm. Leaves flat to channelled, 2–4 mm wide, stiff, dark green. Inflorescence of 2–3 short erect pistillate spikes and 1 staminate spike. Perigynia nerveless, dark brown to nearly black at maturity.
habitat: Mesic to moist tundra, heath, and exposed ridges throughout the Low and High Arctic. A common sedge of drier tundra in the ISR, often co-occurring with Cassiope and Dryas.
distinguishing_features: Short, stiff, erect sedge with dark to nearly black spikelets. Rhizomatous, forming dense patches on drier ground. Distinguished from C. aquatilis by preference for drier habitats and darker, smaller spikelets. Definitive ID requires a hand lens.

photos.attribution: (c) Dmitry Ivanov, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Dmitry Ivanov

growth_form: Densely tufted perennial grass 20–60 cm. Leaves flat or folded, 1–3 mm wide, rough to the touch. Inflorescence a large spreading panicle with shiny 2-flowered spikelets; florets bear a bent twisted awn.
habitat: Moist meadows, streambanks, lake shores, and disturbed ground throughout the Low Arctic. Common across a variety of habitats in the ISR on moist mineral soils.
distinguishing_features: Dense grass tussocks with a large, open, shimmering panicle are distinctive. The bent awn visible with a hand lens. The most common tufted grass forming substantial clumps in open tundra.

photos.attribution: (c) Соколков Юрий Павлович, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Соколков Юрий Павлович

growth_form: Rhizomatous perennial grass 10–35 cm. Leaves flat, 3–7 mm wide, grey-green. Inflorescence a dense cylindrical spike-like panicle 1–3 cm long, grey-white due to long silky hairs on lemmas and glumes. Spikelets 1-flowered.
habitat: Moist to wet tundra, streambanks, and disturbed ground throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common throughout the ISR in moist habitats, especially on nutrient-enriched soils near water and bird activity.
distinguishing_features: Short dense grey-white cylindrical seed head on a leafy stem is distinctive. Silky hairs give the inflorescence a soft woolly texture unlike most Arctic grasses. One of the more recognizable grasses in the region.

photos.attribution: (c) Émilie Desjardins, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Tufted perennial herb 10–25 cm. Leaves grass-like, flat, 2–4 mm wide, with a fringe of long white hairs along margins. Flowers tiny, dark chestnut-brown, in a congested clustered inflorescence at stem tip. Capsules brown, 3-seeded.
habitat: Dry to mesic tundra, rocky slopes, and heath throughout the Low and High Arctic. One of the most common rushes in the ISR on well-drained mineral soils.
distinguishing_features: Grass-like leaves with a conspicuous fringe of white hairs visible to the naked eye, and a dark chestnut-brown clustered inflorescence. Easily separated from grasses and sedges by the hairy leaf margins.

photos.attribution: (c) Игорь Поспелов, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Игорь Поспелов

growth_form: Aquatic perennial 10–50 cm; lower portion submerged, upper aerial and rigid. Leaves in whorls of 6–12, linear, 1–4 cm. Aerial portion erect, resembling a bottle brush. Flowers minute, petal-less, in leaf axils.
habitat: Shallow ponds, lakes, slow streams, and polygon pools throughout the Low Arctic. Very common in standing and slow-moving water throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Distinctive bottle-brush appearance with tight whorls of narrow leaves. No other plant in the region resembles it. Emergent portions stiff; submerged portions soft and flaccid.

photos.attribution: (c) Eric Hough, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Eric Hough

growth_form: Rhizomatous perennial fern ally, dimorphic. Sterile shoots 10–40 cm, green, jointed, with whorled spreading green branches at each node. Fertile shoots appear first in spring, lacking chlorophyll, pale brown, bearing a terminal spore cone (strobilus).
habitat: Moist to wet disturbed ground, riverbanks, roadsides, and tundra throughout the Low Arctic and subarctic. One of the most common and widespread plants in the ISR across a wide range of habitats.
distinguishing_features: Jointed ribbed green stems with whorls of green branches at each node; resembles a miniature pine tree. Pale leafless fertile shoots appear briefly in early spring. The most common horsetail in disturbed habitats.
cultural_notes: Young fertile shoots eaten in spring in some Indigenous traditions. High silica content.

photos.attribution: (c) gwenbezodis, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Tall slender perennial fern ally 30–80 cm. Stems hollow, smooth, 4–10 mm diameter, mostly branchless or with few short branches in the upper portion. Whorls, when present, of short few branches near upper nodes. Emergent from shallow water.
habitat: Shallow ponds, lake edges, and marshes throughout the Low Arctic. Common in standing water throughout the ISR, often forming extensive stands in shallow ponds.
distinguishing_features: The tallest horsetail; mostly branchless or with only a few short branches near the top, growing from standing water. Hollow stems snap cleanly. Distinguished from E. arvense by aquatic habit, taller stature, and minimal branching.

photos.attribution: (c) Герман, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Erect evergreen shrub 30–100 cm. Leaves elliptic to oblong, 2–5 cm, leathery, dark green above, densely rust-woolly below, margins revolute, strongly aromatic when crushed. Flowers white, 5-petaled, in rounded terminal clusters.
habitat: Sphagnum bogs, wet heath, and moist tundra throughout the Low Arctic and boreal. Common in acidic wet substrates throughout the ISR; more abundant in the Mackenzie Delta region.
distinguishing_features: Broader leaves (2–5 cm) than R. tomentosum subsp. decumbens; same rust-woolly underside and strong resinous odour. Both species may co-occur in wet habitats and are best separated by leaf width.
cultural_notes: Leaves widely brewed as tea in Indigenous traditions across the North. Consumed in moderation—contains ledol and grayanotoxins potentially harmful in excess.

photos.attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by agbelliveau

growth_form: Erect to spreading shrub 30–150 cm. Leaves elliptic to oblong, 2–6 cm, green above, grey-glaucous and silky-hairy below, margins entire or obscurely toothed. Stipules small or absent. Catkins 2–5 cm, appearing with leaves.
habitat: Mesic to moist shrub tundra, riverbanks, and slopes throughout the Low Arctic. One of the most common and widespread willows in the ISR, occurring across a broad range of substrates.
distinguishing_features: Grey-glaucous silky leaf undersides and erect growth form distinguish it from most other local willows. Definitive separation from S. pulchra and S. richardsonii requires examination of stipules, catkin characters, and habitat. Among the most variable willows in the region.

photos.attribution: (c) Jacques Ranger, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Low to medium rounded shrub 30–100 cm. Leaves broadly elliptic to nearly round, 2–6 cm, densely woolly on both surfaces when young, becoming greener above with age. Catkins large, 3–7 cm, stout and very woolly, golden-yellow, appearing before leaves.
habitat: Moist shrub tundra, streambanks, and sheltered slopes throughout the Low Arctic. Scattered throughout the ISR; locally common in mesic to moist shrub communities.
distinguishing_features: Densely woolly leaves and conspicuously large, stout, golden-woolly catkins are distinctive. Young leaves appear almost white from heavy wool. Distinguished from S. richardsonii by broader, rounder leaves and golden (not whitish) catkins.

photos.attribution: (c) Bruce Bennett, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Tree or large shrub to 20+ m (shrubby at treeline). Bark grey, furrowed on mature trunks. Leaves ovate, 5–12 cm, dark glossy green above, pale and often resin-stained below, with a flattened petiole. Buds large, resinous, intensely fragrant. Catkins long, drooping.
habitat: Riverbanks, floodplains, and sheltered slopes at the treeline ecotone. Reaches the southern fringe of the ISR along major river corridors; more common and taller south toward Inuvik.
distinguishing_features: The only tree-forming species in the area; unmistakable as a tree in treeline habitats. Large, strongly fragrant resinous buds and glossy leaves diagnostic. Resin scent detectable at a distance in warm weather.
cultural_notes: Resinous bud exudate used as a salve and adhesive in many Indigenous traditions. Inner bark edible in spring.

photos.attribution: (c) Siler Russ, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Low, spreading perennial herb 10–40 cm. Leaves alternate, lanceolate to ovate, 2–6 cm, grey-green, fleshy, glaucous. Flowers large, 4-petaled, deep rose-pink, 2–4 cm, in short few-flowered terminal racemes. Pods narrow, splitting to release plumed seeds.
habitat: Gravel bars, riverbanks, glacial outwash, and disturbed mineral soils throughout the Low Arctic. Abundant along the braided channels of the Mackenzie Delta and coastal gravel areas near Tuktoyaktuk.
distinguishing_features: Large, deep rose-pink flowers on a low, grey-green plant sprawling across gravel are unmistakable. Much shorter and broader-flowered than C. angustifolium; the two rarely share habitat.
cultural_notes: Young shoots, leaves, and stems edible, used similarly to C. angustifolium. Among the most visually striking plants of arctic gravel bars in summer.

photos.attribution: (c) Kayla Brown, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Kayla Brown

growth_form: Robust perennial herb 20–60 cm. Leaves basal and alternate on stem, broadly oblong to ovate, 5–20 cm, on long petioles, with wavy or crisped margins. Inflorescence a dense, branched panicle of small greenish to reddish flowers. Fruiting valves large, heart-shaped, reddish.
habitat: Streambanks, lake shores, wet meadows, and disturbed moist ground throughout the Low Arctic. Scattered but locally common throughout the ISR in moist, often nutrient-rich habitats.
distinguishing_features: The largest-leaved herbaceous plant on wet tundra; broad, wavy-margined dock leaves are unmistakable. Reddish fruiting panicle conspicuous in late summer. Sour taste confirms ID.
cultural_notes: Young leaves eaten raw or cooked as greens; high in vitamin C. Taste similar to Oxyria digyna but larger and less intensely sour.

photos.attribution: (c) Alexey P. Seregin, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Alexey P. Seregin

growth_form: Low perennial herb 3–15 cm, from a deep taproot. Leaves basal, oblanceolate to narrowly lobed, 3–10 cm, often reddish-tinged. Flower heads solitary on hollow scapes, yellow, 1.5–2.5 cm. Seed heads globose, achenes with white pappus.
habitat: Mesic to dry tundra, disturbed ground, beach ridges, and exposed mineral soils throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common throughout the ISR on a wide range of substrates.
distinguishing_features: Low-growing dandelion with smaller flower heads than weedy Taraxacum spp. Reddish leaf tinge is common. Distinguished from introduced T. officinale by smaller stature, shallower leaf lobing, and native habitat preference.
cultural_notes: All parts edible; leaves, flowers, and roots used as food and tea similarly to common dandelion. Young leaves best before flowering.

photos.attribution: (c) Claire Dutilleul, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Erect perennial herb 20–80 cm, spreading from rhizomes. Leaves alternate, 4–10 cm, deeply pinnately lobed, green above, white-woolly below. Flower heads small, numerous, yellowish, nodding, in leafy panicles. Strongly aromatic.
habitat: Disturbed ground, streambanks, nutrient-enriched sites near bird colonies, and roadsides throughout the Low Arctic and subarctic. Common throughout the ISR wherever nitrogen is elevated, including around settlements.
distinguishing_features: Strongly and distinctively aromatic (sage-like) odour on contact. White-woolly leaf undersides with green upper surface. The largest-statured wormwood in the region; often forms dense stands on disturbed ground.
cultural_notes: Widely used medicinally across the Arctic—as a poultice, insect repellent, and in steam treatments. Not consumed internally in significant quantities.

photos.attribution: (c) Nadezhda Liksakova, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Nadezhda Liksakova

growth_form: Erect perennial herb 20–50 cm. Leaves pinnately compound with 11–23 pairs of lanceolate leaflets arranged like ladder rungs. Flowers funnel-shaped, blue-violet with a yellow throat, 1.5–2 cm, in a dense terminal cluster. Pleasantly skunky odour.
habitat: Moist to mesic tundra, streambanks, and shrubby slopes throughout the Low Arctic and subarctic. Locally common throughout the ISR in moderately moist habitats.
distinguishing_features: Ladder-like pinnate leaves and sky-blue funnel flowers with yellow throat are unmistakable. Distinctive faintly skunky fragrance. The only blue-flowered herb of this size in the region.

photos.attribution: (c) sacjackson, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Slender perennial herb 5–20 cm from a creeping rhizome. Basal leaves long-petioled, 3-parted, leaflets deeply lobed. Involucral leaves 3, similar to basal. Flower solitary, yellow, 5 petal-like sepals, 1–1.5 cm. Achene head cylindrical.
habitat: Moist to wet tundra, streambanks, and snowbed margins throughout the Low Arctic. Common throughout the ISR in moist sheltered habitats.
distinguishing_features: The only yellow-flowered anemone in the region; A. parviflora (white, bluish below) is the other common species. Creeping rhizome and 3-parted involucral leaves mid-stem distinguish it from buttercups.

photos.attribution: (c) Andy Fyon, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Andy Fyon

growth_form: Delicate perennial herb 10–30 cm. Basal leaves long-petioled, heart-shaped, 1–3 cm. One clasping stem leaf mid-stem. Flower solitary, terminal, white, 5 petals with green veins, 1.5–2.5 cm. Staminodes (sterile stamens) fringed, resembling nectar glands.
habitat: Fens, wet heath, moist rocky tundra, and streambanks throughout the Low Arctic. Common in calcareous wet habitats throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Single white flower with distinctly green-veined petals and ornate fringed staminodes is unmistakable. The clasping stem leaf is diagnostic. One of the most elegant and distinctive flowers in the Low Arctic.

photos.attribution: (c) Hornbeam Arts, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Perennial herb 5–25 cm. Stems leafy, reddish-hairy. Leaves alternate, narrowly lanceolate, 1–2 cm. Flowers 1–3, yellow, 5-petaled, 1.5–2 cm, petals with orange basal spots. Often solitary on reddish stems.
habitat: Wet sedge meadows, fens, polygon bogs, and boggy tundra throughout the Low Arctic. Common in wet, acidic habitats across the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Yellow flowers with orange basal spots on petals on a reddish-hairy stem in wet tundra are distinctive. The only common yellow-flowered saxifrage of wet habitats in the region.

photos.attribution: (c) Osmonali Bektemir, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Osmonali Bektemir

growth_form: Mat-forming perennial herb 5–20 cm. Leaves crowded on sterile shoots, spatula-shaped, rigid, with 3 sharp teeth at the tip, 5–15 mm. Flowers white with orange or reddish spots, 5-petaled, 8–12 mm, in a few-flowered cyme.
habitat: Dry to mesic rocky tundra, exposed ridges, and gravelly slopes throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common on well-drained mineral and rocky substrates throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Rigid leaves with 3 sharp apical teeth are unmistakable—the prickly texture of the leaf rosette is felt immediately on touch. White spotted flowers on a mat of stiff-leaved cushions distinguish it from other saxifrages.

photos.attribution: (c) Jason Hollinger, some rights reserved (CC BY)

growth_form: Low perennial herb 5–25 cm from a branched caudex. Leaves trifoliate (3 leaflets), leaflets obovate, toothed, dark green above, densely white-woolly below. Flowers yellow, 5-petaled, 1–1.5 cm, in few-flowered cymes.
habitat: Dry to mesic rocky tundra, ridges, and gravelly slopes throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common on well-drained substrates throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Trifoliate leaves with brilliant white-woolly undersides are diagnostic. Distinguished from A. anserina by trifoliate (not pinnate) leaves and smaller stature. One of several yellow-flowered cinquefoils in the region.

photos.attribution: (c) olga_zero, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Erect perennial herb 10–40 cm. Basal leaves simple, ovate, long-petioled; stem leaves pinnately divided. Flowers tiny, pink to white, in a dense rounded terminal head 1–2 cm across. Mildly aromatic.
habitat: Moist to mesic tundra, shrubby slopes, and streambanks throughout the Low Arctic and subarctic. Scattered throughout the ISR in moderately moist habitats.
distinguishing_features: Dense, compact rounded flower head of tiny pink-white flowers atop a leafy stem with pinnately divided upper leaves. The rounded capitate inflorescence is distinctive among arctic forbs.

photos.attribution: (c) Paul Tavares, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Paul Tavares

growth_form: Erect perennial herb 10–25 cm. Leaves alternate, linear to lanceolate, 2–5 cm. Flowers inconspicuous, enclosed within brightly coloured (red to pink-red) bracts; actual petals small, greenish, tubular. Inflorescence a dense spike. Hemiparasitic on roots of grasses and sedges.
habitat: Mesic to moist tundra, heath, and grassy slopes throughout the Low Arctic of the western Canadian Arctic. Documented from the Mackenzie Delta and western ISR.
distinguishing_features: Brightly coloured red to pink-red bracts make this immediately recognizable as an Indian paintbrush. The only Castilleja in the Tuktoyaktuk area; the vivid bract colour is unlike any other local plant.

photos.attribution: (c) Susan Elliott, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Small insectivorous perennial herb 3–15 cm. Leaves in a flat basal rosette, oblong, 1–5 cm, fleshy, yellowish-green, covered in sticky glands that trap small insects. Flowers solitary on leafless scapes, violet-purple, 2-lipped, with a spur, 1–1.5 cm.
habitat: Wet rocky tundra, seepage areas, mossy streambanks, and calcareous fens throughout the Low Arctic. Locally common in wet rocky habitats throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Flat rosette of yellowish, glistening, sticky leaves (clearly insectivorous) and solitary purple-spurred flowers are unmistakable. Leaf surface feels tacky to the touch; small insects often visible stuck to leaves.

photos.attribution: (c) AJC1, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

growth_form: Low perennial herb 5–15 cm. Leaves pinnately lobed, mostly basal, 3–8 cm. Flowers large, creamy-white to pale pink, bilabiate, with a curved hood-like upper lip; clustered in a compact rounded head. Hemiparasitic.
habitat: Mesic to dry tundra, ridges, and heath throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common throughout the ISR on well-drained mineral and gravelly substrates.
distinguishing_features: Compact rounded head of large creamy-white hooded flowers with distinctive recurved upper lip beak. The largest-flowered lousewort in the region; pale colouration contrasts with the pink-purple P. sudetica and P. lanata.

photos.attribution: (c) Logan McLeod, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA), uploaded by Logan McLeod

growth_form: Perennial herb 10–35 cm from a rhizome. Leaves opposite, lanceolate to narrowly elliptic, 3–8 cm, slightly hairy. Stems with 1–3 pairs of leaves. Flower heads 1–3, yellow, with ray and disc florets, 2–4 cm across.
habitat: Mesic to dry tundra, heath, rocky slopes, and open shrub tundra throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common on well-drained substrates throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Yellow daisy-like flower heads with opposite stem leaves distinguish arnicas from superficially similar composites. The narrow leaves and arctic habitat separate it from other yellow-rayed composites in the region.
cultural_notes: Traditionally used as a topical medicinal plant for bruises and inflammation, consistent with the wider ethnobotanical use of Arnica across North America.

photos.attribution: (c) Andy Fyon, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Low perennial herb 3–15 cm. Stem simple, hairy, usually bearing 1 flower head. Basal leaves spatula-shaped, 1–4 cm. Flower head with numerous narrow white to purplish ray florets and a yellow disc, 1–2 cm across.
habitat: Moist to mesic tundra, snowbed margins, and rocky slopes throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common throughout the ISR in a variety of habitats.
distinguishing_features: Low, single-headed daisy with white to purplish-tinged rays and hairy stem. The small stature and arctic habitat narrow identification, but multiple Erigeron species occur in the ISR and definitive separation requires close examination of involucre and ray number.

photos.attribution: (c) Eva Ullström, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Perennial herb 10–30 cm. Stem conspicuously purple-tinged, woolly at base. Basal leaves long-petioled, rounded to ovate, 2–5 cm; stem leaves pinnately lobed. Flower heads 1–several, yellow, with ray florets, 1.5–2 cm.
habitat: Mesic to moist tundra, rocky slopes, and heath throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common on well-drained to moderately moist substrates throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Purple-tinged stem with woolly base and yellow daisy-like heads on a relatively low plant. The purplish stem colouration is distinctive among arctic yellow composites. Formerly treated as Senecio atropurpureus.

growth_form: Tiny annual herb 1–8 cm. Stems reddish, branched. Leaves opposite, ovate, fleshy, 3–8 mm, often reddish-tinged. Flowers minute, whitish-pink, 3-parted, in small clusters in leaf axils. Often overlooked due to small size.
habitat: Wet, cold, late-melting snowbeds, muddy lake margins, and seepage areas throughout the Low and High Arctic. Found in persistently wet cold microsites throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Tiny reddish, fleshy-leaved annual on bare wet mud is unlike any other plant in the region. The diminutive size and reddish colouration make it easy to overlook but distinctive once found.

photos.attribution: (c) Pyak Andrei, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Pyak Andrei

growth_form: Slender perennial herb 5–25 cm, often forming loose mats. Leaves opposite, linear to narrowly lanceolate, 1–3 cm, stiff and erect, shiny green. Flowers white, 5 petals each deeply 2-lobed (appearing as 10), on long slender stalks. Stems 4-angled.
habitat: Mesic to moist tundra, grassy slopes, and disturbed ground throughout the Low Arctic. One of the most common small forbs in the ISR, widespread across habitats.
distinguishing_features: Shiny, stiff, narrowly linear opposite leaves and small white star-like flowers on long pedicels distinguish this from other chickweeds. The deeply 2-lobed petals give the flower a 10-petaled appearance.

photos.attribution: (c) Austin Zeller, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Austin Zeller

growth_form: Low, loosely mat-forming perennial herb 5–20 cm. Leaves opposite, ovate to elliptic, 1–2 cm, hairy. Flowers white, 5 deeply notched petals, 1–1.5 cm; petals slightly longer than sepals. Stems glandular-hairy.
habitat: Rocky tundra, gravelly slopes, and exposed ridges throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common on dry to mesic mineral soils throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Relatively large white flowers with notched petals on a hairy, loosely spreading plant in rocky habitats. Distinguished from Stellaria longipes by broader leaves and glandular-hairy stems. The largest-flowered Cerastium in the region.

photos.attribution: (c) Heather Pickard, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Heather Pickard

growth_form: Small, tufted perennial herb 3–15 cm. Leaves basal, flat, linear, grass-like, 2–8 cm, distichous (in two ranks). Flowering scape slender, bearing a compact terminal raceme of small white to greenish flowers, often tinged red or purple, 3–6 mm.
habitat: Wet rocky tundra, seepage areas, mossy streambanks, and calcareous fens throughout the Low and High Arctic. Locally common in wet habitats across the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Grass-like leaves in two flat ranks with a small, tightly clustered terminal raceme of whitish to reddish flowers on a slender scape. The 2-ranked leaf arrangement is unusual and diagnostic among small monocots of wet tundra.

photos.attribution: (c) Alexander Yakovlev, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Alexander Yakovlev

growth_form: Prostrate to ascending succulent perennial forming mats, 5–25 cm tall. Leaves opposite, fleshy, ovate, 1–2 cm, closely overlapping, pale green. Flowers white, 5 petals, 8–12 mm, on short stalks. Salt-tolerant.
habitat: Sandy and gravelly beaches, coastal dunes, and storm-deposited wrack lines along the Beaufort Sea coast. A characteristic plant of the shoreline near Tuktoyaktuk.
distinguishing_features: Fleshy, pale green, closely overlapping opposite leaves on prostrate stems at the shoreline are unmistakable. The succulent appearance and strictly coastal habitat distinguish it from all other chickweed relatives in the region.

photos.attribution: (c) Дмитрий Мезенин, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Дмитрий Мезенин

growth_form: Slender perennial herb 10–40 cm from a rhizome. Leaves basal, very narrow, semi-cylindrical, 10–30 cm. Flower spike slender, with small greenish flowers on short stalks. Fruit narrow, club-shaped, splitting into 3 slender segments.
habitat: Wet sedge meadows, fens, and waterlogged tundra throughout the Low Arctic. Common in seasonally wet habitats throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Very narrow, semi-cylindrical leaves emerging from wet ground and a slender spike of small greenish flowers. Distinguished from T. maritima by the narrower, club-shaped fruit (not cylindrical). Leaves resemble a grass or rush but lack leaf blades.
cultural_notes: Seeds and young leaves reportedly edible in small quantities but plant contains cyanogenic compounds; caution advised.

photos.attribution: (c) Tyson Ehlers, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Tyson Ehlers

growth_form: Robust perennial herb 15–50 cm. Leaves basal, flat to semi-cylindrical, 10–40 cm, sheathed at base. Flower spike dense, with many small greenish to reddish flowers closely appressed to the stem. Fruit cylindrical, 6-parted.
habitat: Coastal salt marshes, tidal flats, and saline wet meadows throughout the Low Arctic. Common in saline and coastal habitats near Tuktoyaktuk and the Beaufort Sea coast.
distinguishing_features: Denser and more robust than T. palustris; cylindrical 6-parted fruit (not club-shaped 3-parted). Saline coastal habitats. The two arrowgrasses often grow in different zones of the same wetland.
cultural_notes: Contains cyanogenic glycosides—not consumed.

photos.attribution: (c) David Greenberger, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND)

growth_form: Aquatic perennial from a thick rhizome. Leaves floating, oval to heart-shaped, 5–15 cm, deeply notched at base, on long flexible stalks. Flowers solitary, floating or emergent, yellow, 2–4 cm, with 5 large petal-like sepals and many small petals.
habitat: Sheltered lakes, ponds, and slow streams throughout the Low Arctic and subarctic. Locally common in deeper, sheltered lakes in the ISR and Mackenzie Delta.
distinguishing_features: Floating round-to-oval leaves deeply notched at the base and yellow globular flowers are unmistakable. The only water lily in the region. Rhizome very large and spongy.
cultural_notes: Seeds edible when parched or ground; rhizomes edible but require preparation. Used as food in various Indigenous traditions.

photos.attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by 葉子

growth_form: Aquatic or semi-aquatic perennial 10–40 cm. Leaves flat, ribbon-like, 3–8 mm wide, floating or emergent. Flower heads round, spiky, 1–2 cm, borne on a branching stem; upper heads staminate, lower pistillate, becoming bur-like fruits.
habitat: Shallow ponds, lake margins, and slow streams throughout the Low Arctic. Common in shallow standing water throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Ribbon-like floating or emergent leaves and round, spiky, bur-like flower and fruit heads are diagnostic. The only bur-reed in the region; not to be confused with Eriophorum or grasses despite similar leaf shape.

photos.attribution: (c) Svetlana Nesterova, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Svetlana Nesterova

growth_form: Robust aquatic perennial grass 30–80 cm from creeping rhizomes. Leaves flat, 4–8 mm wide, bright green. Inflorescence a large, open, drooping panicle with purplish to yellowish spikelets, 10–20 cm, distinctly nodding.
habitat: Shallow ponds, lake margins, and standing water throughout the Low Arctic. A dominant aquatic grass in the ISR; forms extensive submersed and emergent stands in shallow coastal and polygon ponds.
distinguishing_features: Tall, bright green aquatic grass with a large, distinctly drooping (pendant) purple-tinged panicle. The nodding inflorescence in shallow water is unmistakable. An important waterfowl forage plant.

photos.attribution: (c) Игорь Поспелов, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Slender rhizomatous perennial grass 10–40 cm. Leaves flat, 2–4 mm wide, soft. Inflorescence a narrow, contracted panicle 3–8 cm, with few spikelets; spikelets 2–4 flowered, purplish to brownish. Lemmas with tufts of hairs at base.
habitat: Wet sedge meadows, polygon bogs, and coastal wet tundra throughout the Low and High Arctic. A dominant grass of wet, often coastal, tundra throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Slender rhizomatous grass of wet tundra with a narrow purplish-brown panicle; distinguished from Arctophila fulva by much smaller stature, narrower panicle, and terrestrial rather than aquatic habitat. Definitive ID from similar species requires examination of lemma hair tufts.

photos.attribution: (c) Marie-Ève Garon-Labrecque, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND)

growth_form: Rhizomatous perennial sedge 15–40 cm, non-tussock forming. Leaves very narrow, 1–3 mm, with a triangular tip. Single terminal white to slightly rust-tinged cotton head per stem. Scales reddish-brown with pale midrib.
habitat: Wet sedge meadows, Sphagnum fens, and polygon bogs throughout the Low Arctic. Common in wet, often acidic tundra throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Single cotton head like E. vaginatum but not tussock-forming; rhizomatous and spreading. Reddish-brown scales with pale midrib and rust-tinted cotton diagnostic. Distinguished from E. scheuchzeri by scale colour and habitat preference.

photos.attribution: (c) Sean Blaney, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Sean Blaney

growth_form: Rhizomatous perennial sedge 10–35 cm, non-tussock forming. Leaves very narrow, with a long triangular tip. Single terminal, pure white cotton head, often the largest and most brilliant white of the single-headed cottongrasses. Scales blackish with pale midrib.
habitat: Wet meadows, lake margins, and coastal tundra throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common in the wettest habitats of the ISR, often at lake and pond margins.
distinguishing_features: Pure brilliant white cotton head (largest and whitest of single-headed species) with blackish scales. Non-tussock, rhizomatous growth. Distinguished from E. vaginatum (tussock-forming) and E. russeolum (rust-tinged cotton, reddish-brown scales) by these features.

photos.attribution: (c) Mikael Creative, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND)

growth_form: Slender rhizomatous perennial sedge 10–30 cm. Leaves very narrow, 1–2 mm, shorter than stems. Inflorescence of 2–3 pistillate spikes, loosely and widely spaced, few-flowered and nodding; scales dark purple-black. Staminate spike terminal, slender.
habitat: Wet Sphagnum bogs, fens, and wet sedge meadows throughout the Low Arctic. Common in acidic wet tundra habitats throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Loosely spaced, few-flowered, nodding pistillate spikes with conspicuously dark purple-black scales are diagnostic. More open and nodding than most other arctic sedges. Definitive ID requires a hand lens.

photos.attribution: (c) M. Torre Jorgenson, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by M. Torre Jorgenson

growth_form: Distinctive perennial sedge 10–30 cm. Produces long, cord-like horizontal runners (stolons) lying along the sedge surface, rooting at nodes and producing erect flowering stems. Leaves narrow. Inflorescence of 2–4 sessile, clustered spikelets at stem tip.
habitat: Floating Sphagnum mats, shallow pond margins, and wet fens throughout the Low Arctic. Common in wet acidic habitats throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: The long, string-like stolons connecting rosettes along the surface of Sphagnum or floating mats are unmistakable. No other sedge in the region reproduces by such conspicuous surface runners.

photos.attribution: (c) David GENOUD, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA)

growth_form: Densely tufted perennial grass 5–25 cm. Leaves filiform (thread-like), very narrow (< 1 mm), folded or rolled, short. Inflorescence a narrow contracted panicle 2–5 cm, with purplish-bronze spikelets. Lower sheaths persistent and fibrous.
habitat: Dry to mesic rocky and gravelly tundra, ridges, and heath throughout the Low and High Arctic. Very common on well-drained substrates throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Tight clumps of very fine, short, thread-like leaves with a narrow purplish-bronze panicle. One of the most abundant arctic grasses but requires hand lens for separation from other fine-leaved fescues.

photos.attribution: (c) Matt Reala, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Matt Reala

growth_form: Loosely tufted to spreading perennial grass 5–30 cm, often with bulbils replacing spikelets. Leaves flat, 1–3 mm wide, with a boat-shaped tip. Panicle open, with spreading branches; spikelets 2–5 flowered, often purplish. Lemmas with a webbed tuft of hairs at base.
habitat: Mesic to dry tundra, rocky slopes, and heath throughout the Low and High Arctic. Among the most abundant arctic grasses in the ISR across a wide range of habitats.
distinguishing_features: Boat-shaped leaf tip is characteristic of the genus Poa. Often produces small bulbils in place of flowers, allowing vegetative reproduction—when present, bulbils are distinctive. Separation from other Poa species requires lemma examination.

photos.attribution: (c) Finn McGhee, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Erect rhizomatous perennial grass 30–80 cm. Leaves flat, 2–4 mm wide, rough to the touch. Inflorescence a narrow contracted panicle 5–15 cm, with densely crowded spikelets, purplish-brown. Lemma with a straight awn and conspicuous basal hairs.
habitat: Wet sedge meadows, lake margins, and moist disturbed ground throughout the Low Arctic and subarctic. One of the most common and abundant grasses of wet tundra throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Tall, erect, densely-spiked, purplish-brown panicle in wet habitats. The narrow contracted panicle distinguishes it from the open spreading panicle of Deschampsia cespitosa. Awn and basal hairs visible with hand lens.

photos.attribution: (c) Jukka Jantunen, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Jukka Jantunen

growth_form: Tiny annual or short-lived perennial grass 1–8 cm. Leaves very short, flat, 1–2 mm wide. Inflorescence a narrow spike-like panicle 1–3 cm, with tiny single-flowered spikelets without awns. Often overlooked due to diminutive size.
habitat: Late-melting snowbeds, cold wet depressions, and muddy lake margins throughout the Low and High Arctic. Found in the coldest and wettest microsites throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: One of the smallest grasses in the Arctic. The tiny size, spike-like panicle, and very cold wet habitat are characteristic. Often appears alongside Koenigia islandica. Awnless single-flowered spikelets confirm genus.

photos.attribution: (c) Matt Lavin, some rights reserved (CC BY)

growth_form: Small tufted perennial rush 3–15 cm. Leaves basal, cylindrical, 1–2 mm. Inflorescence a compact head of 2–4 brownish-purple flowers subtended by a long bract that resembles a continuation of the stem. Capsule 3-valved.
habitat: Wet rocky tundra, seepage areas, and snowbed margins throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common in cold wet habitats throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Very small rush with a compact dark head appearing to be pierced by the subtending bract. The extremely compact, few-flowered inflorescence and tiny stature are diagnostic. Distinguished from J. triglumis by typically having 2 rather than 3 flowers in the head.

photos.attribution: (c) Ida B D Jacobsen, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Ida B D Jacobsen

growth_form: Erect, unbranched to forking perennial fern ally 5–20 cm. Stems densely covered in small, stiff, needle-like leaves 3–6 mm, giving a fir-twig appearance. No strobili; spores produced in axils of unmodified leaves. Gemmae (small vegetative propagules) often present in upper leaf axils.
habitat: Heath tundra, mossy rocky slopes, and shrub tundra throughout the Low and High Arctic. Common on well-drained acidic substrates throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Erect, fir-branch-like stems densely covered in stiff needle-like leaves, no specialized cone. Distinguished from Lycopodium annotinum by lack of strobili and non-creeping habit. Small yellowish gemmae in upper leaf axils visible to the naked eye.

photos.attribution: (c) Ramunė Vakarė, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA), uploaded by Ramunė Vakarė

growth_form: Creeping and ascending perennial fern ally; horizontal stems trailing along ground to 50+ cm, with erect annual branches 10–20 cm. Leaves stiff, narrow, spreading or slightly reflexed, 5–8 mm. Strobili (spore cones) solitary, terminal, 2–4 cm, on stalks.
habitat: Heath tundra, mossy slopes, and open shrub tundra throughout the Low Arctic and boreal. Common on acidic, well-drained to moderately moist substrates throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Creeping stems with erect annual branches bearing stiff, spreading, sharp-pointed leaves and a single terminal spore cone are diagnostic. The tufted upright branches from a trailing stem distinguish it from Huperzia selago.

photos.attribution: (c) pulmat, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by pulmat

growth_form: Low, mat-forming fern ally 2–8 cm. Stems creeping, branched, producing small, flat, scale-like leaves in 4 rows (2 larger lateral, 2 smaller dorsal). Strobili small, terminal on ascending branches. Leaves with a minute hair-like tip.
habitat: Dry rocky tundra, gravelly ridges, and exposed slopes throughout the Low Arctic. Locally common on dry, exposed mineral substrates throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Flat, fern-frond-like branching with four rows of minute scale-like leaves distinguish spikemosses from true mosses and all clubmosses. Very low, mat-forming habit on dry rocky ground is characteristic.

photos.attribution: (c) V.S. Volkotrub, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by V.S. Volkotrub

growth_form: Delicate, small perennial herb 3–10 cm, often producing stolons. Basal leaves kidney-shaped, 3–5 lobed, 5–15 mm, long-petioled. Flowers 1–3, white, 5-petaled, 5–8 mm. Stems and petioles reddish.
habitat: Cold seepage areas, streambanks, and late-melting snowbeds throughout the Low and High Arctic. Found in the coldest, wettest rocky microsites throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Very small delicate herb with kidney-shaped, lobed basal leaves on reddish stems in cold seepage habitats. Distinguished from S. cernua by absence of bulbils and even smaller stature. Requires close examination to separate from other small saxifrages.

photos.attribution: (c) rpernick, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by rpernick

growth_form: Low cushion-forming perennial herb 3–15 cm. Leaves basal in a dense rosette, spatula-shaped, 5–15 mm, hairy. Flowering stems leafless or with 1–2 leaves. Flowers yellow, 4-petaled, in a compact raceme. Fruit a flat, oval silicle 5–10 mm.
habitat: Dry to mesic gravelly tundra, ridges, and rocky slopes throughout the Low and High Arctic. One of the most common and widespread Draba species throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Low basal rosette of hairy leaves with a leafless stem bearing a compact cluster of small yellow 4-petaled flowers. The Draba genus contains many similar species in the Arctic requiring detailed examination; D. alpina is among the most frequently encountered.

photos.attribution: (c) Shawn Billerman, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Shawn Billerman

growth_form: Perennial herb 10–25 cm. Leaves pinnately lobed, borne in whorls of 3–4 at intervals along the stem (not alternate), giving a distinctive tiered appearance. Flowers pink to purple, bilabiate, in a dense terminal spike. Hemiparasitic.
habitat: Mesic tundra, heath, and rocky slopes throughout the Low Arctic. Scattered throughout the ISR in well-drained habitats.
distinguishing_features: Leaves arranged in whorls of 3–4 along the stem rather than alternately is unique among local Pedicularis species. The tiered whorl arrangement is visible to the naked eye and immediately distinguishes this species.

photos.attribution: (c) M. Goff, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA), uploaded by M. Goff

growth_form: Small insectivorous perennial herb 5–25 cm. Leaves in a flat basal rosette, round to spatula-shaped, 5–12 mm wide on long petioles, covered in reddish glandular tentacles tipped with sticky glistening droplets that trap insects. Flowers white, tiny, on a slender leafless scape.
habitat: Sphagnum bogs, wet heaths, and acidic fens throughout the Low Arctic and boreal. Present in Sphagnum-dominated wet habitats in the southern ISR and Mackenzie Delta region.
distinguishing_features: Round leaves covered in reddish, dew-drop-tipped tentacles on a flat rosette in Sphagnum are unmistakable. The glistening, sticky glands are clearly visible and functionally capture insects. The only sundew in the region.

photos.attribution: (c) Vladimir Bryukhov, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Low, acaulescent (stemless) perennial herb 5–15 cm, from a branched caudex. Leaves pinnately compound, basal, with 15–25 lanceolate leaflets, silky-hairy. Flowers bright yellow, pea-like, in a short compact raceme on a leafless scape. Pods erect, hairy.
habitat: Dry to mesic rocky and gravelly tundra, ridges, and well-drained slopes throughout the Low Arctic. Common on mineral substrates throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Compact, stemless plant with silky pinnate leaves and bright yellow pea-flowers on a leafless scape. Distinguished from Hedysarum alpinum by yellow (not pink-purple) flowers and hairy erect (not loment) pods. Caution: genus Oxytropis contains toxic species.
cultural_notes: Some Oxytropis species contain swainsonine and are toxic to livestock. Not consumed.

photos.attribution: (c) Wren Coxson, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Wren Coxson

growth_form: Straggling semi-aquatic perennial herb 15–40 cm, from creeping rhizomes. Leaves pinnately compound, 5–7 leaflets, toothed, green above, greyish below. Flowers deep wine-red to purple, 5-petaled, 1.5–2 cm, with large dark-purple sepals exceeding the petals.
habitat: Sphagnum bogs, fens, shallow pond margins, and wet sedge meadows throughout the Low Arctic and boreal. Common in wet, acidic to moderately fertile habitats throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Deep wine-red to dark purple flowers with large sepals are unlike any other cinquefoil in the region—all other local species have yellow flowers. Semi-aquatic habit in Sphagnum or wet sedge habitats is consistent.

photos.attribution: (c) arthur_haendler, some rights reserved (CC BY)
